text: fat is 81.1g. saturated fat is a major pressure point. for this category, the real question is fat quality.
carbs is 0.1g. outside of that, the profile is pretty quiet. not relevant here.
protein is 0.9g. everything else is fairly underwhelming. also not relevant here.
vitamin e at 11% DV, vitamin a at 28% DV, vitamin k at 6% DV stand out most. that is barely moving the needle.
minerals are basically not part of the case here.

pros first. simple traditional fat because this gives butter a cleaner identity than heavily processed fat substitutes. very strong culinary usefulness because butter has real kitchen legitimacy and practical use that matters in this category. can fit as a moderate traditional fat because the editorial position here is that butter belongs as a flawed but legitimate middle-ground fat rather than a bottom-tier industrial product.

now the downsides. saturated fat is a major drawback because this is the main reason butter cannot be an elite oils-and-fats food. cholesterol is high because a second meaningful strike against the fat profile. fat profile is clearly weaker than top-tier oils because butter is not competing with the best oils on chemistry alone.
B tier. Best in controlled amounts where fat quality is the main reason to use it.
